claim: The LNG-EUR/USD channel structurally constrains ECB monetary policy space via ~$40-50B/year structural USD demand from Europe's shift to USD-denominated LNG, shifting TTF-EUR/USD correlation from r=-0.15 to r=-0.45
needs: Validation from FX and macro specialists; independent corroboration of the correlation shift magnitude; assessment of whether LNG supply diversification will moderate the effect

claim: Gold at $3,000+ prices structural loss of monetary policy credibility rather than cyclical real-rate dynamics, with $400-600/oz residual above real-rate-implied levels
needs: Decomposition of the gold-real rate residual between fiscal dominance attribution and post-Russia sanctions reserve diversification; assessment of central bank buying persistence and concentration

claim: The Fed framework review (2025-2026) is mispriced event risk with ~50% non-event probability
needs: Historical base rate analysis of prior framework review outcomes; assessment of institutional inertia strength; analysis of whether SEP dot dispersion widening (50bp to 125bp+) signals genuine framework instability

claim: China's monetary/fiscal policy mix is the single most important driver of base metals outcomes given 55% copper consumption share and rotation toward copper-intensive applications
needs: Broader corroboration from macro and commodity specialists; quantification of demand rotation magnitude (steel to copper-intensive); assessment of China's property sector drag on overall commodity demand

claim: The credit-equity basis (equity-implied spreads 20-40bp wider than actual credit spreads) signals credit market overoptimism with historical 2006-07 precedent
needs: Assessment of whether private credit ($1.7T) structurally changes the signal reliability; timing analysis given the basis can persist 12-18+ months; verification that the Merton-model conversion parameters are appropriate for current market structure

claim: Dollar weaponization creates a parallel monetary transmission channel bypassing central banks via OFAC compliance burden and bank self-sanctioning
needs: Quantitative assessment of the regulatory tax magnitude on cross-border intermediation; evidence on whether self-sanctioning extends meaningfully beyond comprehensively sanctioned countries; decomposition of EM policy space compression between sanctions-driven and traditional rate differential factors
